summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on how his experience with detailed code review in open source communities shaped his approach to feedback, and why that approach failed when he became a startup CEO. In open source, thorough feedback on even trivial code was welcomed because it invested in the author's growth within a volunteer community of craftspeople who assumed mutual respect. In corporate settings, the same detailed feedback was received as micromanagement and criticism because the context and implicit messages differ fundamentally. Rocklin concludes that people can only absorb about one piece of feedback per interaction in corporate contexts, requiring feedback to be more selective, higher-level, and carefully framed.
key_points: Good feedback educates the author and invests in their future, not just critiques or provides direction
Open source code review works because of volunteerism, craft focus, assumed respect, and public accountability
The same detailed feedback that signals investment in OSS signals criticism and micromanagement in corporate contexts
Corporate workers don't identify as deeply with skills like writing or meeting facilitation as OSS devs do with coding
People can absorb only about one piece of feedback per interaction in corporate settings, requiring selectivity
money_quotes: Writing code is a vanishingly small fraction of software development.
Critique without constructive direction is just complaining, and adds constraint to the author without also helping with momentum.
The main reason for good feedback is to invest in the author.
What before was a supportive behavior is now an abusive one. Everything depends on context.
In volunteer OSS if people don't like you they just ignore you. Anyone reviewing your work is doing so because they care and want to help you.
I've learned that a person can absorb only about one piece of feedback per interaction, and then only if it's labeled very specifically as feedback with the best intentions.
This miscommunication isn't the receiver's fault. We as reviewers need to better model our context so we can anticipate how feedback will be received.
key_insight: Feedback that signals investment and respect in one context can signal criticism and hostility in another, making context awareness essential for effective communication.
